IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of what NMT models learn about morphology and provides systematic, cross-linguistic empirical evidence (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Hebrew) using POS/morphological tagging on encoder and decoder representations. Key findings—strong encoder morphological representations, limited decoder morphological learning, the impact of attention on encoder/decoder roles, and advantages of character-CNNs—are likely to influence how researchers analyze and design NMT architectures and subword modeling. These results open clear follow-up directions (improving decoder morphology, leveraging encoder features) and are likely to be adopted by those working on NMT interpretability and morphologically-rich language translation. | B(4): The paper tackles an important and timely problem—what NMT models learn about morphology—using systematic, crosslinguistic experiments (Arabic, German, Czech, French, Hebrew) and extrinsic POS/morphological probing. Its findings (encoder representations encode morphology much better than decoder; attention reduces decoder morphological signal; character-CNNs yield stronger morphological representations; depth and target language matter) directly inform model design and interpretation, so they are likely to change how researchers probe and build NMT systems. The work opens clear follow-ups (improving decoder-side morphology, attention designs, multilingual probing) and its probing methodology has broader applicability beyond MT.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of what NMT models learn about morphology and provides systematic, cross-linguistic empirical evidence (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Hebrew) using POS/morphological tagging on encoder and decoder representations. Key findings—strong encoder morphological representations, limited decoder morphological learning, the impact of attention on encoder/decoder roles, and advantages of character-CNNs—are likely to influence how researchers analyze and design NMT architectures and subword modeling. These results open clear follow-up directions (improving decoder morphology, leveraging encoder features) and are likely to be adopted by those working on NMT interpretability and morphologically-rich language translation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—what NMT models learn about morphology—using systematic, crosslinguistic experiments (Arabic, German, Czech, French, Hebrew) and extrinsic POS/morphological probing. Its findings (encoder representations encode morphology much better than decoder; attention reduces decoder morphological signal; character-CNNs yield stronger morphological representations; depth and target language matter) directly inform model design and interpretation, so they are likely to change how researchers probe and build NMT systems. The work opens clear follow-ups (improving decoder-side morphology, attention designs, multilingual probing) and its probing methodology has broader applicability beyond MT.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper studies what NMT models learn about morphology using quantitative probing (POS/morph tagging) across multiple morphologically-rich language pairs, encoder vs decoder, attention, and char vs word representations—topics squarely within NLP/ML conference scope. It uses standard MT data (WIT3/IWSLT), BLEU reporting, and established probing methodology (training classifiers on frozen representations), matching expected methods/evaluation. The work is aimed at the NLP/ML audience interested in model analysis and representation learning and is positioned with relevant prior work (e.g., Shi et al., Adi et al.), making it a strong fit for a top venue. | B(5): The paper clearly addresses core NLP/ML topics—analysis of neural MT representations and what they capture about morphology—which is squarely within top-tier conference scope. It targets the right audience (NMT and representation-learning researchers) and uses standard problem formulation (extrinsic POS/morphological tagging of frozen encoder/decoder features) and datasets (WIT3/IWSLT, gold/predicted tags). Experimental methodology (word vs. char representations, attention ablation, encoder vs. decoder, multiple morphologically-rich language pairs) and positioning against relevant prior work (e.g., Shi et al., Johnson et al.) match expectations for a top venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper studies what NMT models learn about morphology using quantitative probing (POS/morph tagging) across multiple morphologically-rich language pairs, encoder vs decoder, attention, and char vs word representations—topics squarely within NLP/ML conference scope. It uses standard MT data (WIT3/IWSLT), BLEU reporting, and established probing methodology (training classifiers on frozen representations), matching expected methods/evaluation. The work is aimed at the NLP/ML audience interested in model analysis and representation learning and is positioned with relevant prior work (e.g., Shi et al., Adi et al.), making it a strong fit for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly addresses core NLP/ML topics—analysis of neural MT representations and what they capture about morphology—which is squarely within top-tier conference scope. It targets the right audience (NMT and representation-learning researchers) and uses standard problem formulation (extrinsic POS/morphological tagging of frozen encoder/decoder features) and datasets (WIT3/IWSLT, gold/predicted tags). Experimental methodology (word vs. char representations, attention ablation, encoder vs. decoder, multiple morphologically-rich language pairs) and positioning against relevant prior work (e.g., Shi et al., Johnson et al.) match expectations for a top venue.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper has a recognizable structure (data, encoder/decoder analysis, related work) and clearly states its high-level goal of analyzing NMT representations, but the flow is disrupted by numerous duplicated sentences, formatting artifacts, and abrupt repetitions (e.g., repeated paragraphs in Related Work and Word representations). Notation is minimal and generally defined (ENC, DEC, ENCi(s)), but presentation of figures/tables is inconsistent and poorly formatted (Table and Figure references are present but the table rendering and placement are messy), which reduces readability. Overall the ideas are communicated, but significant copy-editing and cleanup are needed to remove redundancy and fix formatting for clearer presentation. | B(3): The paper has a reasonable high-level organization (intro, data, encoder/decoder analysis, related work, experiments) and key terms/notations like ENC/DEC and ENCi(s) are introduced and used consistently. However, presentation is marred by numerous formatting and duplication issues (e.g., repeated paragraphs in Related Work, repeated lines about MT data and character CNN), typographical artifacts (e.g., "ArabicHebrew", the broken word "morphol-"), and inline footnote clutter that make sections harder to follow. Figures/tables are referenced (Figure 1, Tables 1/3/4) and the encoder/decoder analysis is explained clearly, but the noisy repetition and editing problems significantly reduce overall clarity and polish.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper has a recognizable structure (data, encoder/decoder analysis, related work) and clearly states its high-level goal of analyzing NMT representations, but the flow is disrupted by numerous duplicated sentences, formatting artifacts, and abrupt repetitions (e.g., repeated paragraphs in Related Work and Word representations). Notation is minimal and generally defined (ENC, DEC, ENCi(s)), but presentation of figures/tables is inconsistent and poorly formatted (Table and Figure references are present but the table rendering and placement are messy), which reduces readability. Overall the ideas are communicated, but significant copy-editing and cleanup are needed to remove redundancy and fix formatting for clearer presentation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper has a reasonable high-level organization (intro, data, encoder/decoder analysis, related work, experiments) and key terms/notations like ENC/DEC and ENCi(s) are introduced and used consistently. However, presentation is marred by numerous formatting and duplication issues (e.g., repeated paragraphs in Related Work, repeated lines about MT data and character CNN), typographical artifacts (e.g., "ArabicHebrew", the broken word "morphol-"), and inline footnote clutter that make sections harder to follow. Figures/tables are referenced (Figure 1, Tables 1/3/4) and the encoder/decoder analysis is explained clearly, but the noisy repetition and editing problems significantly reduce overall clarity and polish.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies the now-common probing-classifier methodology (e.g. Shi et al. 2016; Adi et al. 2016; Qian et al.) to study morphology in NMT, so the core idea is not novel. Its contributions are nonetheless a non-trivial extension: systematic cross-linguistic focus on morphologically-rich languages, analysis of both encoder and decoder representations (including the surprising decoder weakness), and investigation of attention and char-CNN vs word-based representations (sections 4–5 and 5.1). Overall this is an incremental but useful empirical extension rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation. | B(3): The paper is an empirical analysis rather than a new method: it applies classifier-probing of NMT encoder/decoder representations for POS/morphology, extending prior probing work (e.g. Shi et al. 2016) to morphologically-rich languages, to both source and target sides, and to character-CNN vs word representations. The authors explicitly position it relative to prior work (Shi et al.; Vylomova et al.) and add non-trivial empirical dimensions (attention vs. no-attention, encoder vs. decoder, language-pair variation), but the contribution is primarily an incremental, systematic study rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies the now-common probing-classifier methodology (e.g. Shi et al. 2016; Adi et al. 2016; Qian et al.) to study morphology in NMT, so the core idea is not novel. Its contributions are nonetheless a non-trivial extension: systematic cross-linguistic focus on morphologically-rich languages, analysis of both encoder and decoder representations (including the surprising decoder weakness), and investigation of attention and char-CNN vs word-based representations (sections 4–5 and 5.1). Overall this is an incremental but useful empirical extension rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is an empirical analysis rather than a new method: it applies classifier-probing of NMT encoder/decoder representations for POS/morphology, extending prior probing work (e.g. Shi et al. 2016) to morphologically-rich languages, to both source and target sides, and to character-CNN vs word representations. The authors explicitly position it relative to prior work (Shi et al.; Vylomova et al.) and add non-trivial empirical dimensions (attention vs. no-attention, encoder vs. decoder, language-pair variation), but the contribution is primarily an incremental, systematic study rather than a fundamentally new idea.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a broad empirical study: multiple language pairs (including several morphologically rich ones and both translation directions), word vs. character representations, encoder vs. decoder analyses, attention ablation, classifier variants, and use of gold vs. predicted tags — providing solid evidence for its claims. It also discusses negative/ surprising results (weak decoder representations) and offers hypotheses. Minor gaps remain: some reproducibility details are deferred to supplementary material and the excerpt shows no experiments on alternate subword methods or systematic data-size sensitivity, so the evaluation is strong but not exhaustively comprehensive. | B(4): The paper runs a broad set of experiments across multiple language pairs (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Arabic–Hebrew, Arabic–German) and evaluates encoder vs. decoder representations, word vs. character models, and the role of attention (including attention/no-attention ablations). It uses both predicted and gold tags where available, reports BLEU alongside tagging accuracy (e.g. Table 4), and situates the work well in related work. The authors acknowledge and analyze negative results (poor decoder representations) and test hypotheses (attention removal), but some reproduction details (full hyperparameters, exact tagger setups) are deferred to supplementary material and are not present in the excerpt, leaving modest gaps in sensitivity/reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a broad empirical study: multiple language pairs (including several morphologically rich ones and both translation directions), word vs. character representations, encoder vs. decoder analyses, attention ablation, classifier variants, and use of gold vs. predicted tags — providing solid evidence for its claims. It also discusses negative/ surprising results (weak decoder representations) and offers hypotheses. Minor gaps remain: some reproducibility details are deferred to supplementary material and the excerpt shows no experiments on alternate subword methods or systematic data-size sensitivity, so the evaluation is strong but not exhaustively comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments across multiple language pairs (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Arabic–Hebrew, Arabic–German) and evaluates encoder vs. decoder representations, word vs. character models, and the role of attention (including attention/no-attention ablations). It uses both predicted and gold tags where available, reports BLEU alongside tagging accuracy (e.g. Table 4), and situates the work well in related work. The authors acknowledge and analyze negative results (poor decoder representations) and test hypotheses (attention removal), but some reproduction details (full hyperparameters, exact tagger setups) are deferred to supplementary material and are not present in the excerpt, leaving modest gaps in sensitivity/reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper situates itself among relevant prior work (e.g. Shi et al. (2016) and Vylomova et al. (2016)) and cites key methods for handling morphology (char-CNN, BPE/word-piece) in Related Work. It performs controlled, fair comparisons (same WIT3 data, dev/test sets, encoder vs. decoder, attention ablation, word- vs. char-based representations; reports majority and linear classifier baselines, Table 4 and §5.1). The paper does not, however, present direct quantitative head-to-head results against prior published representation-analysis numbers (beyond qualitative contrasts), a minor omission given its empirical focus. | B(4): The paper situates itself with and cites relevant recent work (e.g. Shi et al. 2016 as most similar, Vylomova et al. 2016) and compares different modeling choices (word vs. char CNN, encoder vs. decoder, attention vs. no-attention) using the same WIT3 data and POS/morph tagging evaluation, including gold vs. predicted tags, which supports fair comparisons. It analyzes and acknowledges prior analyses and the division of labor between encoder/decoder rather than claiming unjustified improvements. Minor omissions: it does not present direct numeric comparisons to prior published baselines for POS/morph tagging or alternative subword approaches (e.g. BPE/word-piece) in the same evaluation, which would make the comparison set more comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper situates itself among relevant prior work (e.g. Shi et al. (2016) and Vylomova et al. (2016)) and cites key methods for handling morphology (char-CNN, BPE/word-piece) in Related Work. It performs controlled, fair comparisons (same WIT3 data, dev/test sets, encoder vs. decoder, attention ablation, word- vs. char-based representations; reports majority and linear classifier baselines, Table 4 and §5.1). The paper does not, however, present direct quantitative head-to-head results against prior published representation-analysis numbers (beyond qualitative contrasts), a minor omission given its empirical focus.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper situates itself with and cites relevant recent work (e.g. Shi et al. 2016 as most similar, Vylomova et al. 2016) and compares different modeling choices (word vs. char CNN, encoder vs. decoder, attention vs. no-attention) using the same WIT3 data and POS/morph tagging evaluation, including gold vs. predicted tags, which supports fair comparisons. It analyzes and acknowledges prior analyses and the division of labor between encoder/decoder rather than claiming unjustified improvements. Minor omissions: it does not present direct numeric comparisons to prior published baselines for POS/morph tagging or alternative subword approaches (e.g. BPE/word-piece) in the same evaluation, which would make the comparison set more comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims (encoder representations capture morphology well; decoder representations are weaker; character-based inputs improve morphological signals; attention shifts where information is stored) are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence (multi-language WIT3 training, encoder/decoder feature extraction, POS/morph classifiers, and attention vs. no-attention ablation as reported in Tables/Section 5.1). There are no mathematical derivations to check. Minor methodological concerns remain: the excerpt lacks details on classifier capacity, statistical significance testing, and some experiments rely on predicted tags and teacher-forcing of target words (which the authors note) and could confound interpretation of decoder representations. These do not undermine the main empirical conclusions but merit further controls/ reporting. | B(4): The paper’s main claims (that encoder representations capture morphology better than decoder representations, that character-based input improves tagging, and that attention changes the encoder/decoder division of labor) are directly tested with reasonable experiments: frozen pretrained encoders/decoders used to extract word features, POS/morphology classifiers trained on gold/predicted tags, and an attention ablation study (see §4–5 and Table 4). There are no mathematical derivations to check. However, methodological details are missing in the provided text (classifier architectures/hyperparameters, dataset sizes for each probe, and no mention of statistical significance), and the decoder analysis may be confounded by the use of teacher-forcing (decoder given gold target words) and by relying often on predicted tags; these omissions are minor but leave some interpretive claims (about attention ‘hurting’ decoder representations) less conclusively supported.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims (encoder representations capture morphology well; decoder representations are weaker; character-based inputs improve morphological signals; attention shifts where information is stored) are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence (multi-language WIT3 training, encoder/decoder feature extraction, POS/morph classifiers, and attention vs. no-attention ablation as reported in Tables/Section 5.1). There are no mathematical derivations to check. Minor methodological concerns remain: the excerpt lacks details on classifier capacity, statistical significance testing, and some experiments rely on predicted tags and teacher-forcing of target words (which the authors note) and could confound interpretation of decoder representations. These do not undermine the main empirical conclusions but merit further controls/ reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (that encoder representations capture morphology better than decoder representations, that character-based input improves tagging, and that attention changes the encoder/decoder division of labor) are directly tested with reasonable experiments: frozen pretrained encoders/decoders used to extract word features, POS/morphology classifiers trained on gold/predicted tags, and an attention ablation study (see §4–5 and Table 4). There are no mathematical derivations to check. However, methodological details are missing in the provided text (classifier architectures/hyperparameters, dataset sizes for each probe, and no mention of statistical significance), and the decoder analysis may be confounded by the use of teacher-forcing (decoder given gold target words) and by relying often on predicted tags; these omissions are minor but leave some interpretive claims (about attention ‘hurting’ decoder representations) less conclusively supported.
